# Stimulation, Sustenance, Subversion: The General Education Board and Southern US Public Education

Matthew D. Davis

Pages 313-322 | Published online: 07 Dec 2006

* Cite this article
* <https://doi.org/10.1080/00220620600984230>

* Full Article
* Figures & data
* Citations
* Metrics
* Reprints & Permissions
* Read this article /doi/full/10.1080/00220620600984230?needAccess=true

## Abstract

This article explores the General Education Board, a John D. Rockefeller‐led philanthropy, and its work during the first half of the twentieth century to improve Southern US public education. In contrast to most historical treatments of the Board that limit their investigations to its origin, this essay explores the full sweep of the Board’s history. In addition, this essay suggests three particular efforts of the General Education Board in its work to improve Southern US public education: stimulation, sustenance, and subversion.

Previous article View issue table of contents Next article

## Notes

1\. On the General Education Board’s origins, see Bullock, Henry Allen. _A History of Negro Education in the South: From 1619 to the Present_ . Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1967; Link, W. A. _A Hard Country and a Lonely Place: Schooling, Society, and Reform in Rural Virginia, 1870–1920_ . Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1986; Anderson, J. _The Education of Blacks in the South, 1860–1935_ . Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1985; Anderson, E., and A. Moss, Jr. _Dangerous Donations: Northern Philanthropy and Southern Black Education, 1902–1930_ . Columbia: University of Missouri Press, 1999; and Watkins, W. _The White Architects of Black Education, Ideology and Power in America, 1865–1954_ . New York: Teachers College Press, 2001.

2\. A notable exception to this negative analysis of the GEB can be found in Anderson and Moss, _Dangerous Donations_ .

3\. For administrative progressives, see for example, Tyack, D. _The One Best System: A History of American Urban Education_ . Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1974.

4\. Wallace Buttrick in: Heffron, M. “Nation‐Building for a Venerable South: Moral and Practical Uplift in the New Agriculture Education, 1900–1920.” In _Essays in Twentieth‐Century Southern Education: Exceptionalism and Its Limits_ , edited by W. J. Urban. New York: Garland Publishing, Inc., 1997, 63.

5\. Sallee, Shelley. _The Whiteness of Child Labor Reform in the New South_ . Athens: University of Georgia Press, 2004, 83.

6\. On _new education_ in America, see Heffron, “Nation‐Building”, 43–46. “New Education: Genesis and Metamorphoses” served as the focus for the 2004 meeting of the International Standing Conference for the History of Education where an earlier draft of this essay was presented.

7\. Fosdick, R. B. _Adventures in Giving: The Story of the General Education Board, A Foundation Established by John D. Rockefeller_ . New York: Harper & Row Publishers, 1962, 125. Attention to state departments of education in the history of Southern education remains sparse.

8\. I thank Rockefeller Archive Center Senior Archivist Thomas Rosenbaum for offering this concept as an important element to the Rockefeller philanthropic efforts.

9\. See, for example, Anderson, _The Education of Blacks in the South._

10\. I thank Professor Wayne Urban for noting this anomaly.

11\. See, for examples, Bonner, T. N. _Iconoclast: Abraham Flexner and a Life in Learning_ . Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2002 ; Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 115–22.

12\. Fleming, L. E. “The General Education Board’s Influence on Southern Education from 1905.” _Journal of the Midwest History of Education Society_ 23 (1996): 114.

13\. See, for examples, Ibid.; and Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ .

14\. For a report of the struggle toward accreditation of white schools in one state, see LeCompte, K. N., and O. L. Davis, Jr. “Establishment of Academic Standards for Early Twentieth Century Texas High Schools: The University of Texas Affiliated Schools Program.” _Journal of Educational Administration and History_ 37, no. 1 (April 2005): 71–84. See also Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ .

15\. See, for example, LeCompte and Davis, “Establishment of Academic Standards”.

16\. Fleming, “The General Education Board’s Influence”, 112.

17\. This support began in 1906. Ibid., 113.

18\. Davis, M. D. “Advocates and Overseers: State Agents of Negro Education in the Pre‐Brown South.” Unpublished paper presented at the 2004 American Educational Research Association annual meeting, San Diego, CA, April 2004.

19\. Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 77.

20\. Ibid.

21\. Davis, M. D. “‘Attuned to the Art of the Possible’: The GEB’s Jackson Davis.” _American Educational History Journal_ 34, no. 2 (2004): 124–28.

22\. Apparently, no women served in these posts. Similarly, and unsurprisingly, the GEB’s own policy hierarchy exhibits this gender disparity.

23\. “Conference of the State Superintendents of Education in the South with Officers of the General Education Board.” Folder 4288, Box 408, Subseries 3, Series 1, General Education Board Papers, Rockefeller Archives Center, Sleepy Hollow, New York.

24\. S. L. Smith, the Rosenwald Fund’s Southern Director, recognized and appreciated in 1931 the GEB’s broad effort as supporting state departments of education. See letter, S.L. Smith to Albert Stern, 25 November 1931, File 11, Box 128, Rosenwald Fund Collection, Fisk University Special Collections, Nashville, Tennessee.

25\. Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 125.

26\. Ibid.

27\. “Fred McCuistion”, in “GEB Personnel” loose leaf binder (107), Rockefeller Archives Center, Sleepy Hollow, New York. While no careful exploration of the GEB fellowship program has been accomplished, a similar study has been undertaken of the Rosenwald Fund’s fellowship program. See Beilke, Jayne R. “The Changing Emphasis of the Rosenwald Fellowship Program, 1928–1948.” _Journal of Negro Education_ 66, no. 1 (Winter 1997): 3–15.

28\. The GEB, along with the Phelps–Stokes Fund and the Carnegie Corporation, collaborated on many of these efforts. While no historical overview has explored the breadth of this ‘Atlantic Crossings’ (Rogers, D.T. _Atlantic Crossings: Social Politics in a Progressive Age_ . Cambridge: Belknap Press, 2000) knowledge transfer on educating Africans and African Americans, several studies have investigated this phenomenon principally from an America to Britain (and its empire) perspective. See, for examples, Heyman, R. D. “The Role of Carnegie Corporation in African Education, 1925–1960.” Ph.D. diss, Columbia University, 1970; Murphy, E. Jefferson. _Creative Philanthropy: Carnegie Corporation and Africa, 1953–1973_ . New York: Teachers College Press, 1976; and King, K. J. _Pan‐Africanism and Education: A Study of Race, Philanthropy, and Education in the Southern States of America and East Africa_ . Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1971.

29\. On acknowledgement of this professionalization project, see, for example, Goodenow, R. K. “Paradox in Progressive Educational Reform: The South and the Education of Blacks in the Depression Years.” _Phylon_ 39, no. 1 (1978): 50.

30\. Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 125.

31\. Notes from Jackson Davis interview with South Carolina State Superintendent Hope, File 1106, Box 127, Subseries 1, Series 1, General Education Board Papers, Rockefeller Archives Center, Sleepy Hollow, New York. See also Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 125.

32\. “GEB Resolution No. 289”, 30 June 1932, File 2053, Box 213, Subseries 2, Series 1, General Education Board Papers, Rockefeller Archives Center, Sleepy Hollow, New York.

33\. This support is acknowledged but not analyzed in Saylor, J. G. _Factors Associated with Participation in Cooperative Programs of Curriculum Development_ . New York: Teachers College, 1941. See also “Conference of the State Superintendents of Education in the South with Officers of the General Education Board”, Folder 4288, Box 408, Subseries 3, Series 1, General Education Board Papers, Rockefeller Archives Center, Sleepy Hollow, New York.

34\. See note 1.

35\. Many of these actions are explored in Davis, M. D. “Curriculum Leadership for the Jim Crow South: The General Education Board between the two World Wars.” _Curriculum & Teaching Dialogue_ (in press).

36\. Davis, M. D. “The GEB and Institutionalization of Black Public Schooling.” _American Educational History Journal_ 33, no. 2 (2006): 71–78.

37\. Fosdick, _Adventures in Giving_ , 324.

38\. Davis, “Attuned to the Art of the Possible”.

39\. Walker, V. S. “Organized Resistance and Black Educators’ Quest for School Equality, 1878–1938.” _Teachers College Record_ 107, no. 3 (March 2005): 355–88.

40\. Ibid., 379. As well, Dixon kept the Georgia Teachers and Education Association ‘from bankruptcy’ during this period of time. See Fairclough, A. _Teaching Equality: Black Schools in the Age of Jim Crow_ . Athens: University of Georgia Press, 2001, 58.

41\. Bullock’s _A History of Negro Education in the South_ explores the GEB only until the mid‐1920s. Similarly, Link, in _A Hard Country_ , stops his narrative in 1920; Anderson and Moss, in their _Dangerous Donations_ , stop in 1930; and Anderson, in _The Education of Blacks in the South_ , stops in 1935.

42\. Davis, M. D. “Behind‐the‐Scenes Ally: The GEB, Southern Black High Schools, and Inter‐war Curriculum Reform.” _Curriculum History–2004_ (College Station, 2005).

43\. A strong body of scholarship is emerging about the Jeanes Supervisors. See, for example, Chirhart, Ann Short. _Torches of Light: Georgia Teachers and the Coming of the Modern South_ . Athens: University of Georgia Press, 2005.

44\. For a rare recognition of the hierarchical relationship between the GEB and the Rosenwald Fund, see Smith, C. Calvin. _Educating the Masses: The Unfolding History of Black School Administrators in Arkansas, 1900–2000_ . Columbia: University of Missouri Press, 2003, 5.

45\. Davis, “The GEB and Institutionalization of Black Public Schooling”.

46\. For example, see notes about Rebecca Davis in Smith, S. L. _Builders of Goodwill_ . Nashville: Tennessee Book Co., 1950, 49; and the NASC Interim History Writing Committee. _The Jeanes Story: A Chapter in the History of American Education, 1908–1968_ . Jackson, 1979. Davis’ story remains an under‐recognized remarkable story of educational travels from Alabama to Arkansas to Libya to Missouri and, finally, to Georgia.

47\. See, for examples, the essay on Anna M. P. Strong in Smith, _Educating the Masses_ , 43; and Hicks, W. N. _et al_ . “Annie Wealthy Holland.” In _Five North Carolina Negro Educators_ , edited by N. C. Newbold. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1939, 61–85.

48\. For the story of one principal and community stalwart that lost his job as integration was finally accepted, see Walker, V. S. _Their Highest Potential: An African American School Community in the Segregated South_ . Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1996. See also Walker, V. S., and Ulysses Bryas. “The Architects of Black Schooling in the Segregated South: The Case of One Principal Leader.” _Journal of Curriculum & Supervision_ 19, no. 1 (Fall 2003): 54–72.

49\. Conley, D. J. “Philanthropic Foundations and Organizational Change: The Case of the Southern Education Foundation (SEF) During the Civil Rights Era.” Ph.D. diss., Northwestern University, 1990.
